questions.question: Which field task is considered the single most important in trauma care?
questions.rationale: Airway control is paramount because hypoxia is rapidly fatal and cannot wait for hospital arrival.

questions.question: The preferred maneuver to open the airway in a trauma patient is:
questions.rationale: The modified jaw thrust opens the airway without moving the cervical spine, protecting a potential spinal injury.

questions.question: Minute volume is best described as:
options: Tidal volume multiplied by respiratory rate
questions.rationale: Minute volume = tidal volume × respiratory rate, representing the total air moved per minute.

questions.question: An oropharyngeal airway (OPA) should be used only in patients who:
options: Have a suspected basilar skull fracture
questions.rationale: An OPA can stimulate the gag reflex and induce vomiting; it is reserved for patients with no gag reflex.

questions.question: A nasopharyngeal airway is relatively contraindicated when there is suspicion of:
questions.rationale: With basilar skull fracture, an NPA (or nasal intubation) risks passing into the cranial vault.

questions.question: What oxygen flow rate is used with a nonrebreather mask?
questions.rationale: A nonrebreather is run at 15 L/min to keep the reservoir filled and deliver a high oxygen concentration.

questions.question: The recommended bag-valve-mask ventilation rate for an apneic adult is:
questions.rationale: Adults are ventilated at 8–10 breaths per minute with oxygen, confirming chest rise to avoid hyperventilation.

questions.question: The most reliable method to confirm and monitor endotracheal tube placement is:
questions.rationale: Waveform capnography provides continuous confirmation of tube placement and ventilation; auscultation alone is unreliable.

questions.question: A blind insertion airway device (BIAD) is best described as:
options: A device requiring direct cord visualization
A supraglottic airway placed without seeing the cords
questions.rationale: A BIAD is a supraglottic airway inserted blindly, useful as a primary or rescue airway.

questions.question: The mnemonic MMAP is used to predict:
questions.rationale: MMAP (Mallampati/mouth opening, Mask seal/obstruction/obesity, Anatomy, Position) anticipates difficult mask ventilation and intubation.

questions.question: Apneic oxygenation refers to:
options: Delivering oxygen (e.g., nasal cannula) during the apneic intubation attempt
questions.rationale: Apneic oxygenation keeps oxygen flowing during laryngoscopy to prolong safe apnea time and prevent desaturation.

questions.question: External laryngeal manipulation (ELM/BURP) is performed to:
options: Improve the glottic view during intubation
questions.rationale: ELM applies backward, upward, rightward pressure on the larynx to bring the cords into view and improve first-pass success.

questions.question: A major hazard of ventilating too fast or with excessive volume is:
options: Gastric insufflation and decreased venous return
questions.rationale: Over-aggressive ventilation distends the stomach (aspiration risk) and raises intrathoracic pressure, reducing venous return and cardiac output.

questions.question: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) differs from rapid sequence airway (RSA) in that RSA:
options: Places a supraglottic device instead of an ET tube
questions.rationale: RSA uses the same sedation/paralysis approach but places a supraglottic (BIAD) airway rather than an endotracheal tube.

questions.question: Adequate tidal volume during BVM ventilation is best confirmed by:
questions.rationale: Visible, symmetric chest rise and fall confirms adequate tidal volume; rate and force of squeezing do not.

questions.question: An unrecognized esophageal intubation is dangerous primarily because:
options: It is rapidly fatal from lack of ventilation/oxygenation
questions.rationale: An esophageal tube delivers no ventilation; if unrecognized it is rapidly fatal—hence mandatory capnographic confirmation.

questions.question: Routine hyperventilation of the head-injured patient is:
options: Not recommended except for signs of cerebral herniation
questions.rationale: Hyperventilation reduces cerebral perfusion; it is reserved only for patients showing signs of cerebral herniation.

questions.question: Before the first intubation attempt, the provider should always:
options: Have a backup airway plan and stocked airway kit
questions.rationale: Preparation—including a backup plan and a complete airway kit—is essential before any attempt.

questions.question: Pulse oximetry and capnography respectively measure:
questions.rationale: Pulse oximetry reflects oxygenation (SpO2); capnography reflects ventilation (end-tidal CO2) and confirms tube placement.

questions.question: When repeated intubation attempts fail and the patient is hypoxic, the immediate priority is to:
options: Ventilate with BVM or insert a BIAD
questions.rationale: Failed intubation in a hypoxic patient demands a return to effective oxygenation/ventilation via BVM or a rescue BIAD.
